rules.description: An A2A Agent Card exposes internal capabilities or admin-level skills that should not be advertised externally.
rules.remediation: Limit Agent Card capabilities to public-facing skills only.
rules.title: Agent Card exposes internal capabilities

rules.description: An A2A Agent Card does not require authentication for interactions.
rules.remediation: Add authentication requirements (OAuth 2.0 or API key) to Agent Card.
rules.title: Agent Card lacks authentication requirement

rules.description: A2A skill definitions lack input schemas, allowing unvalidated data to be passed between agents. Unvalidated cross-agent payloads are the canonical ASI08 Agent Communication Poisoning primitive.
rules.remediation: Define explicit JSON schemas for all skill inputs.
rules.title: No input schema validation in A2A skill definitions

rules.description: An A2A agent endpoint uses HTTP instead of HTTPS.
rules.remediation: Use HTTPS for all A2A endpoints.
rules.title: A2A endpoint using HTTP instead of HTTPS

rules.description: An A2A Agent Card configures a JWT token lifetime greater than 1 hour (3600 seconds). Long-lived tokens increase the window for token theft and replay attacks.
rules.remediation: Set JWT token lifetime to 1 hour (3600 seconds) or less. Use refresh tokens for longer sessions.
rules.title: JWT token lifetime exceeds 1 hour

rules.description: An A2A Agent Card disables JWT signature verification or allows the 'none' algorithm, permitting token forgery.
rules.remediation: Enable signature verification and restrict algorithms to RS256 or ES256. Never allow 'none'.
rules.title: Weak JWT validation configuration

rules.description: An A2A Agent Card lacks an 'id' or 'identity' field, or uses an HTTP endpoint, making it susceptible to agent impersonation attacks.
rules.remediation: Add a unique 'id' or 'identity' field to the Agent Card and use HTTPS endpoints.
rules.title: Agent impersonation risk

rules.description: Two agents establish an A2A connection where only the caller authenticates. The callee is trusted by URL alone — easy to spoof.
rules.remediation: Require mutual TLS or dual-bearer auth for all A2A flows.
rules.title: A2A connection lacks mutual authentication

rules.description: An A2A agent forwards incoming delegation tokens without reducing scope, allowing an N-deep chain to accumulate the caller's full rights.
rules.remediation: Reduce delegation scope at each hop; refuse to forward tokens that are already delegated beyond a small bound.
rules.title: Unbounded delegation in A2A call chain

rules.description: An A2A agent trusts claims relayed by a peer without verifying the original issuer. 'Agent B says A says X' must not be treated as X.
rules.remediation: Require signed attestations from the original issuer; do not trust relayed claims.
rules.title: Transitive trust accepted in A2A

rules.description: An A2A flow accepts tokens without nonce, timestamp, or jti checks, allowing captured messages to be replayed. Replayed agent messages are a core ASI08 Agent Communication Poisoning primitive.
rules.remediation: Include jti/nonce and iat/exp claims; reject duplicates inside a replay window.
rules.title: A2A tokens not anti-replay protected

rules.description: An A2A endpoint accepts messages without version discriminator, allowing a v1 payload to be interpreted by a v2 handler (or vice versa) with changed field semantics. Schema-confusion injection is an ASI08 Agent Communication Poisoning pattern.
rules.remediation: Require an explicit schema version in every message; reject mismatches.
rules.title: A2A schema confusion between major versions

rules.description: An agent instruction file (AGENTS.md, .cursorrules, CLAUDE.md) contains shell commands or execution directives that could be injected into agent behavior.
rules.remediation: Remove shell commands from agent instruction files. Use proper tool definitions instead.
rules.title: Agent instruction file contains shell command directives

rules.description: Agent instruction files reference external URLs that could serve as C2 channels or data exfiltration endpoints.
rules.remediation: Remove external URL references from agent instructions.
rules.title: Agent instructions reference external URLs

rules.description: Agent instructions contain patterns that attempt to disable or bypass security controls (e.g., 'ignore security warnings', 'skip verification').
rules.remediation: Remove security override instructions. Agents should respect default security controls.
rules.title: Agent instructions override security controls

rules.description: Agent instruction files reference credentials, API keys, or environment variables.
rules.remediation: Remove credential references from instruction files.
rules.title: Agent instructions contain credential references

rules.description: Agent instruction files contain hidden content via HTML comments, zero-width characters, or Unicode tricks that may manipulate agent behavior covertly.
rules.remediation: Remove hidden content. All agent instructions should be human-readable.
rules.title: Agent instruction file contains hidden content

rules.description: Repository declares a dependency on the upstream Anthropic / ModelContextProtocol SDK (Python `mcp` / `modelcontextprotocol`, TS `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`, Java `io.modelcontextprotocol:*`, Rust `mcp` / `modelcontextprotocol`) and exposes a STDIO transport (`StdioServerTransport`, `stdio_server`, etc.) without a sanitizer on argv assembly. Anthropic declined to CVE this as working as designed — sanitization is the developer's responsibility. The OX Security disclosure on 2026-04-15 rolled up LiteLLM, LangChain and IBM LangFlow as downstream casualties of exactly this pattern. See also AAK-STDIO-001 for the sink-level detector.
rules.remediation: Wrap every argv the STDIO transport builds in an allow-list sanitizer — `shlex.quote` in Python, `execFile` with an explicit argv array in Node, equivalent in Java/Rust. OR switch the transport off STDIO (`transports=['http']` / `['sse']`). If you have deliberately accepted the risk, add `accepts_stdio_risk: true` plus a `justification:` field in `.agent-audit-kit.yml`.
rules.title: MCP server built on the upstream SDK without STDIO sanitizer

rules.description: TimBroddin/astro-mcp-server <=1.1.1 builds SQL queries from `request.params.arguments` via string concatenation in `src/index.ts` (the MCP-tool query-construction path). CVE-2026-7591 (NVD 2026-05-01, CWE-89): no upstream patch released as of the AAK ship date — the latest npm publish (1.1.1) is the same version flagged as the vulnerable ceiling. Two detector arms: a pin-check on package.json / package-lock / yarn.lock / pnpm-lock fires whenever the package is present (because every published version is vulnerable), and a TS / JS source detector fires when files importing the package build queries via string concatenation or untagged template literals. Tagged-template SQL helpers (`sql\`...\``, drizzle, prisma, postgres-js) escape interpolations safely and are intentionally not matched.
rules.remediation: Pin away from astro-mcp-server until a fixed release is published (track upstream at https://github.com/TimBroddin/astro-mcp-server). For the source shape, switch to a parameterized-query API (e.g., `db.query(sql, [param])`, `cursor.execute(sql, params)`) or wrap interpolations in a tagged-template SQL helper such as `drizzle-orm`, `postgres-js`, or `sql-template-tag` — those escape values safely and AAK ignores them.
rules.title: astro-mcp-server SQL injection (CVE-2026-7591, npm <=1.1.1)

rules.description: An `.mcp.json` / `.azure-mcp/` config references an Azure MCP server endpoint without an `Authorization:` header, mTLS client cert, or Azure-AD token-exchange. CVE-2026-32211 (CVSS 9.1) documented the server-side default of no auth on the MCP endpoint; downstream agents must add a transport-layer credential or risk session-hijack / tool-impersonation by anyone reachable on the network.
rules.remediation: Add an `Authorization` header with an Azure-AD token (preferred), an mTLS client certificate, or a static API key obtained from a secrets vault. Azure-AD managed identities or workload identity federation are the documented production paths.
rules.title: Azure MCP server consumed without authentication

rules.description: Repository publishes an Azure-MCP-shaped server (`@azure/mcp-server`, `azure-mcp-server` Python package, or `mcp-server-azure` keywords in `pyproject.toml` / `package.json`) and exposes one or more `/mcp/*` route handlers without an auth middleware on the same route. CVE-2026-32211 (CVSS 9.1) is the server-side default that AAK-AZURE-MCP-001 catches on the consumer side; this rule is the upstream pair so server authors ship secure defaults.
rules.remediation: Add an auth middleware to every `/mcp/*` route — Azure-AD JWT validation, `client_credentials`, mTLS, or a vault-issued API key checked at request time. Reject unauthenticated requests with HTTP 401 *before* dispatching to the MCP handler.
rules.title: Azure MCP server published without auth middleware on /mcp routes

rules.description: On Windows, Claude Code prior to 2.1.75 loads `%ProgramData%\ClaudeCode\managed-settings.json` without validating directory ownership or ACLs. A low-privileged user can plant a malicious config that executes on every launch. CVE-2026-35603 (CVSS 5.4 MEDIUM, CWE-426 Untrusted Search Path).
rules.remediation: Upgrade Claude Code to 2.1.75 or later. If the directory must exist for deployment, ship a sibling `setup.ps1` that runs `icacls` to restrict ACLs to TrustedInstaller + administrators before any Claude Code launch.
rules.title: Claude Code < 2.1.75 reads managed-settings.json from unsafe ProgramData path

rules.description: Meta-rule: fires when all four CrewAI 0.x exploit-chain shapes are reachable in the same module — CVE-2026-2275 (CodeInterpreterTool unsafe_mode), CVE-2026-2285 (JSON loader path traversal), CVE-2026-2286 (RAG SSRF) and CVE-2026-2287 (missing Docker liveness gate). ThaiCERT 2026-04-02 + CERT/CC VU#221883 demonstrate that an untrusted prompt walks the chain to host RCE without further exploitation.
rules.remediation: Apply each child-rule's remediation. The single hardest gate is CVE-2026-2275 (CodeInterpreterTool unsafe_mode); fixing that one breaks the chain. The runtime helpers in `agent_audit_kit.sanitizers.crewai` collectively suppress all four sub-rules.
rules.title: CrewAI four-CVE exploit chain reachable in one module

rules.description: `CodeInterpreterTool(unsafe_mode=True)` drops into a host Python interpreter where ctypes / os.system are reachable. CVE-2026-2275 is the canonical sandbox-escape primitive in the CrewAI 0.x chain (ThaiCERT 2026-04-02 / CERT/CC VU#221883).
rules.remediation: Set `unsafe_mode=False` on every CodeInterpreterTool invocation. If you must run untrusted code, wrap with a real container sandbox and call `agent_audit_kit.sanitizers.crewai.assert_codeinterp_safe_mode(False)` in the same function to suppress this rule.
rules.title: CrewAI CodeInterpreterTool with unsafe_mode=True

rules.description: `JSONSearchTool(file_path=...)` / `JSONLoader(path=...)` accepts an attacker-influenceable path without anchoring to a project root. CVE-2026-2285: untrusted JSON-loader path lets the agent read arbitrary files (chained with the other three CVEs into host RCE).
rules.remediation: Anchor every JSON-loader path with `agent_audit_kit.sanitizers.crewai.validate_jsonloader_path(path, root=...)` before passing into the tool. Calling the validator in the same function suppresses this rule.
rules.title: CrewAI JSON loader path traversal

rules.description: `RagTool(url=...)` / `WebsiteSearchTool(url=...)` accepts a non-constant URL reachable from an untrusted source without an allow-list / private-network guard. CVE-2026-2286: cloud-metadata or loopback SSRF feeds back into the agent tool-use loop.
rules.remediation: Wrap every RAG / website URL with `agent_audit_kit.sanitizers.crewai.validate_rag_url(url, allowlist=[...])`. The helper rejects private IPs after DNS resolution and enforces an explicit hostname allow-list.
rules.title: CrewAI RagTool / WebsiteSearchTool SSRF

rules.description: `CodeInterpreterTool(...)` does not gate execution on a Docker liveness check (`docker_required=True` is unset and `require_docker_liveness(client)` is not called). A dead Docker daemon silently falls back to the host Python interpreter, completing the chain to host RCE (CVE-2026-2287).
rules.remediation: Either set `docker_required=True` on CodeInterpreterTool or call `agent_audit_kit.sanitizers.crewai.require_docker_liveness(client)` in the same function — the helper raises before the tool ever sees the host fallback.
rules.title: CrewAI sandbox fallback without Docker liveness check

rules.description: A function that targets DeepSeek V4 (OpenAI-compatible client with `base_url=` containing 'deepseek', or `import deepseek`) reads from an untrusted source (request body, document loader, file read) and passes the value into a `tools=[{description: ...}]` payload without calling `sanitize_tool_description`. DeepSeek V4 (Apache 2.0, 2026-04-24) exposes MoE routing via its tool-call envelope — untrusted text inside a tool description can poison expert selection (LLM01 with MoE-specific surface). Speculative shape until corpus refresh.
rules.remediation: Pipe untrusted tool descriptions through `agent_audit_kit.sanitizers.deepseek.sanitize_tool_description` before assembling the `tools=` payload. The sanitiser strips ANSI / control characters and routing-poison tokens ([ROUTE: ...], <|route_id|>, __route__=, etc.) and truncates to a max length. Calling it in the same function suppresses this rule.
rules.title: DeepSeek V4 MoE-routed tool description injection

rules.description: The upstream MCP Python, Java, Apollo and TypeScript SDKs shipped a StreamableHTTP transport that trusts the browser-supplied `Host` header. A malicious web page that a user visits can resolve a custom domain to 127.0.0.1 via DNS rebinding and reach a local MCP server, turning every browser tab into a remote-attack surface for stdio-grade tools. The upstream patch adds a Host allow-list; downstream servers embedding StreamableHTTP must enforce one too. See CVE-2025-66414 / CVE-2025-66416 (Python), CVE-2026-35568 (Java), CVE-2026-35577 (Apollo).
rules.remediation: Wrap the StreamableHTTP app with a Host-header allow-list. In Starlette / FastAPI attach `TrustedHostMiddleware(allowed_hosts=...)`; in Node attach an `allowedHosts:` option or a Host middleware; in Java/Apollo enable `HostHeaderFilter` / `allowedHosts` config. Alternatively upgrade the SDK to a patched version and pass through its host-validation option.
rules.title: MCP StreamableHTTP transport without Host-header allow-list

rules.description: A project dependency manifest (requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, package.json, pom.xml, build.gradle) pins an MCP SDK at a version below the DNS-rebinding fix. Patched versions: Python `mcp` >= 1.23.0, TS `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` >= 1.21.1, Java `io.modelcontextprotocol.sdk:mcp-core` >= 0.11.0, `@apollo/mcp-server` >= 1.7.0. Even if the project never serves over StreamableHTTP itself, transitive servers built on the SDK inherit the bug.
rules.remediation: Bump the SDK to the patched version listed in the rule title. If a bump is not yet possible, ensure every transport surface has its own Host-header allow-list (see AAK-DNS-REBIND-001 remediation).
rules.title: Vulnerable MCP SDK version pinned (DNS-rebinding fix missing)

rules.description: Project depends on `apache-doris-mcp-server` at a version < 0.6.1. CVE-2025-66335 is a query-context neutralization bypass in the MCP adapter's tool layer — crafted tool arguments are concatenated into Doris SQL without a parameterized boundary, letting an LLM-driven tool call reach into arbitrary reads/writes. Fixed in 0.6.1.
rules.remediation: Upgrade `apache-doris-mcp-server` to 0.6.1 or newer. Audit every tool the adapter exposes to confirm arguments flow through a parameterized query builder, never string concatenation.
rules.title: apache-doris-mcp-server < 0.6.1 SQL injection

rules.description: Project depends on `excel-mcp-server` at a version <= 0.1.7. CVE-2026-40576 is a path-traversal in the server's `get_excel_path()` helper — absolute paths pass through unchecked, relative paths are joined without resolving-and-validating the result. Combined with the default 0.0.0.0 bind + zero authentication on SSE / Streamable-HTTP, any unauthenticated network peer can read, write or overwrite files anywhere on the host. Fixed in 0.1.8.
rules.remediation: Upgrade `excel-mcp-server` to 0.1.8 or later. Until the bump is in, bind the server to 127.0.0.1 and front it with an auth proxy.
rules.title: excel-mcp-server <= 0.1.7 path traversal

rules.description: Package manifest depends on `flowise` or `flowise-components` at version < 3.1.0, and/or a Flowise flow config (`.flowise/*.json`, `flows/*.json`) declares an MCP adapter node with `customFunction` or `runCode` sinks. CVE-2026-40933 (GHSA-c9gw-hvqq-f33r, CVSS 10.0) lets an authenticated attacker combine allowlisted commands like `npx` with execution flags such as `-c` to achieve arbitrary OS command execution. Same architectural class as Ox's original STDIO disclosure (see AAK-STDIO-001).
rules.remediation: Upgrade flowise and flowise-components to 3.1.0 or later. Audit every MCP adapter node in your flow configs; remove `customFunction`/`runCode` sinks unless they're validated against a strict argv allowlist. See also AAK-STDIO-001 for the architectural-class detector.
rules.title: Flowise < 3.1.0 MCP adapter authenticated RCE

rules.description: A workflow in `.github/workflows/` uses a third-party Action (`owner/action@ref`) where `ref` is a tag or branch name instead of a 40-character commit SHA. A repo-takeover of the Action's publisher can re-point the tag to a malicious revision — the downstream repo consuming it will happily run the new code with `GITHUB_TOKEN` and write permissions. GitHub's April 2026 Security Roadmap ships Immutable Actions and makes SHA pinning the default policy.
rules.remediation: Repin third-party Actions to a 40-character commit SHA and add a `# v1.2.3`-style trailing comment for humans. First-party Actions under `actions/` and `github/` are exempt (they now ship Immutable Actions). Dependabot will auto-bump SHA pins when `update-type: all` is set.
rules.title: Third-party GitHub Action not pinned by full commit SHA

rules.description: A tool description, SKILL.md body, agent card, or system prompt represents the AI as a therapist / counselor / psychologist / 'qualified mental health professional'. Tennessee SB 1580 (signed 2026-04-01) makes this unlawful and enforceable via the TN Consumer Protection Act, with a **private right of action** and $5,000 civil penalty per violation.
rules.remediation: Rewrite the description. The AI can support mental-wellness use cases but cannot claim or imply it is (or replaces) a licensed mental-health professional. Add an explicit 'not a substitute for licensed care' disclaimer.
rules.title: AI described as a mental-health professional (Tennessee SB 1580)

rules.description: Code or prompt describes an AI system making prior-authorization or medical-necessity decisions without licensed-physician review. Kansas, Washington, and Utah 2026 laws require a clinician in the loop for these decisions.
rules.remediation: Route every prior-auth / medical-necessity output through a licensed-physician review step; expose that review in the audit log.
rules.title: AI makes prior-authorization / medical-necessity decisions alone

rules.description: Code / prompt describes an AI system approving or denying insurance coverage without a human in the loop. Georgia and Iowa 2026 laws restrict AI-only coverage / benefit determinations.
rules.remediation: Require human sign-off on coverage decisions; disclose AI involvement to the consumer.
rules.title: AI-only insurance coverage decision

rules.description: Text mentions patient / clinical / mental-health / therapy / diagnosis but the tool never explicitly says the responder is an AI. Multiple 2026 state laws (TN, WA, UT) expect clear AI disclosure in clinical interactions.
rules.remediation: Add a visible 'You are talking to an AI; this is not medical advice and is not a substitute for licensed care' disclosure.
rules.title: Healthcare context without explicit AI-disclosure to user

rules.description: A healthcare AI surface mentions suicide / self-harm / crisis but never references 988 / 911 / 112 / 999 / a crisis line. Tennessee HB 1951 (2026) creates criminal liability for encouraging suicide; lacking an escalation path materially worsens the exposure.
rules.remediation: Add explicit crisis-line escalation instructions in the prompt / system message; test for the most common suicide / self-harm phrases and escalate before generating any other reply.
rules.title: Crisis keywords handled without escalation path

rules.description: A hook command contains network-capable tools (curl, wget, nc, etc.). Hooks run automatically and can exfiltrate code, API keys, or session data.
rules.remediation: Remove network calls from hooks. Use file-based logging if audit trail is needed.
rules.title: Hook executes network-capable command

rules.description: A hook command accesses credential environment variables. This enables direct credential theft via hook execution.
rules.remediation: Hooks should never reference credential environment variables.
rules.title: Hook command contains environment variable exfiltration

rules.description: A hook command writes to paths outside the project boundary, which can modify system configs, plant persistence, or stage exfiltration.
rules.remediation: Constrain all hook file operations to project directory.
rules.title: Hook command writes to files outside project directory

rules.description: A non-formatting hook is attached to a sensitive lifecycle event (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit). These events fire on every tool call or session start.
rules.remediation: Audit all hooks on critical lifecycle events. Use deny-lists for non-formatting commands.
rules.title: Hook on security-sensitive lifecycle event

rules.description: A hook command contains base64 operations, commonly used to obfuscate exfiltration payloads or encode stolen credentials.
rules.remediation: Remove base64 operations from hooks unless there's a documented, legitimate use case.
rules.title: Hook command uses base64 encoding/decoding

rules.description: A hook command uses sudo, doas, pkexec, or chmod +x. Hooks should never require elevated privileges.
rules.remediation: Hooks should never require elevated privileges.
rules.title: Hook command runs with elevated privileges

rules.description: More than 15 hook definitions in a single settings file. Large hook surface increases audit burden and risk of hidden malicious hooks.
rules.remediation: Minimize hooks to essential operations only.
rules.title: Excessive number of hooks defined

rules.description: A hook command contains hex-encoded strings, unicode escapes, very long commands, or nested shell invocations. Obfuscation is a strong indicator of malicious intent.
rules.remediation: All hook commands should be human-readable. Reject obfuscated commands.
rules.title: Hook command contains obfuscated or encoded payload

rules.description: A hook command reads or references project source code files, which could enable code exfiltration.
rules.remediation: Hooks should not access source files. Use dedicated build tools instead.
rules.title: Hook command references project source files

rules.description: A Claude Code hook script command-string interpolates a variable or captured group directly into a shell command. This is the CVE-2025-59536 shape: a poisoned config file triggers arbitrary code execution.
rules.remediation: Never interpolate hook input into a shell string. Use an argv list and a no-shell exec. Quote with shlex.quote if a shell is absolutely required.
rules.title: Hook command interpolates user-controlled input

rules.description: A hook script invokes subprocess.run / spawn with shell=True (or the equivalent in Node/Bash) while passing a composed string that includes caller-provided fields.
rules.remediation: Use shell=False and pass argv as a list. If a shell is needed, build commands from quoted constants only.
rules.title: Hook runs with shell=True and variable interpolation

rules.description: A settings.local.json or project-relative hook file executes before the Claude Code trust prompt. This is the core CVE-2025-59536 regression pattern; any project-contained hook must not run until trust is confirmed.
rules.remediation: Require explicit trust confirmation before loading project-local hook configuration. Upgrade to Claude Code 1.0.111 or later.
rules.title: Hook trust check is bypassable by project-local config

rules.description: A 12-digit Aadhaar number passed the Verhoeff checksum and is embedded in project text. Aadhaar is restricted under the UIDAI Act and India DPDP §8(4). Storing in code is a reportable breach.
rules.remediation: Remove the Aadhaar number. If Aadhaar is genuinely needed, route it through an encrypted vault (e.g. AWS KMS / Azure Key Vault) and never log or commit it.
rules.title: Aadhaar number in source / config

rules.description: A 10-char PAN (5 letters + 4 digits + 1 letter) was detected. PAN is tax-linked personal data under DPDP §8(4).
rules.remediation: Remove the PAN. If needed for processing, tokenize it and store tokens, not raw PANs.
rules.title: PAN (Permanent Account Number) in source / config

rules.description: A UPI VPA (<handle>@<psp>) was detected. UPI IDs are payment identifiers regulated by NPCI and covered by DPDP §8(4).
rules.remediation: Remove the UPI ID. Accept UPI addresses as runtime input only.
rules.title: UPI ID in source / config

rules.description: An IFSC code (4 letters + 0 + 6 alnum) was detected. IFSC is a banking identifier; pairing it with an account number constitutes DPDP §8(4) 'sensitive personal data'.
rules.remediation: Move IFSC codes out of source; look them up at runtime from RBI's public IFSC directory.
rules.title: IFSC code in source / config

rules.description: An Indian +91 mobile number (starting 6/7/8/9) was detected. Phone numbers are personal data under DPDP §8(4).
rules.remediation: Remove the phone number. Never log raw phone numbers — hash them.
rules.title: Indian mobile number in source / config

rules.description: An Indian state-issued vehicle registration (e.g. 'MH 12 AB 1234') was detected. Vehicle registrations are PII in combination with driver records.
rules.remediation: Remove the registration from source. If dealing with vehicle data, anonymize before checking in.
rules.title: Indian vehicle registration in source / config

rules.description: A scanner module crashed during execution. The scan continued with the remaining scanners, but results may be incomplete. This is always a bug in agent-audit-kit itself; please file an issue with the evidence string.
rules.remediation: File an issue at https://github.com/sattyamjjain/agent-audit-kit/issues with the scanner name, exception class, and (if safe) the project shape.
rules.title: Scanner module raised an exception

rules.description: A source / config file (`.md`, `.txt`, `.yml`, `.yaml`, `.json`, `.py`) embeds a known wild IPI payload from the 2026-04-24 Help Net Security + Infosec Magazine catalogue. Common shapes: ignore-prior + exfil, system-role override, reveal-system-prompt, credential exfil via cURL, tool-call rerouting, delete-repository, admin role escalation, obfuscated prompt break, image-attached IPI, RAG-poisoned document. Refresh the corpus with `aak corpus update --ipi`.
rules.remediation: Remove the payload from the file. If the file is intentionally an attack-corpus fixture, exclude it via `--ignore-paths`. The real risk is checked-in poisoned templates / system-prompt files / RAG seed corpora — those need to be sanitized at ingestion time, not at scan time.
rules.title: Indirect-prompt-injection wild payload checked into repo

rules.description: A dependency file pins langchain or langchain-core to a version earlier than 1.2.22. CVE-2026-34070 allows absolute paths and '..' traversal via load_prompt() / load_prompt_from_config().
rules.remediation: Upgrade to langchain-core >= 1.2.22. If you must keep the legacy behavior, pass allow_dangerous_paths=True explicitly.
rules.title: Project depends on LangChain < 1.2.22 (load_prompt path traversal)

rules.description: Source code calls langchain.load_prompt() or load_prompt_from_config() with a user-controlled path argument. Even on patched versions, the allow_dangerous_paths escape hatch is a sharp edge.
rules.remediation: Treat load_prompt() as a file read against a trusted root. Resolve, normalize, and verify the path is inside the intended directory.
rules.title: Call to load_prompt without allow_dangerous_paths review

rules.description: A dependency file pins langchain / langchainjs to a version vulnerable to GHSA-r399-636x-v7f6 / CVE-2025-68664, a serialization-injection chain that extracts secrets through crafted saved chains.
rules.remediation: Upgrade to the patched LangChain / langchainjs release; avoid loading serialized chains from sources you do not fully control.
rules.title: LangChain deserialization of untrusted data

rules.description: `langchain.prompts.load_prompt(path)` / `PromptTemplate.from_file(path)` accepts an attacker-influenced path without anchoring to a project root or allow-listed URI scheme. CVE-2026-34070 (CVSS 7.5): the lc:// scheme + raw file paths let a crafted prompt template read arbitrary files. Patched in `langchain-core>=0.3.74`; pinning a vulnerable version is also a finding.
rules.remediation: Validate every prompt path with `agent_audit_kit.checks.path_under_root(path, root)` before calling load_prompt. For S3 / HTTP backed prompt stores, prefer the explicit `langchain_community.storage.*` adapters; AAK exempts those via pattern-not-inside. Bump `langchain-core` to >= 0.3.74.
rules.title: LangChain load_prompt path traversal (CVE-2026-34070)

rules.description: A function calls a known SSRF guard helper (`validate_safe_url`, `is_safe_url`, `validateSafeUrl`, etc.) and then fetches the same URL via `requests.get`, `httpx.get`, `urllib.request.urlopen`, `fetch`, or similar without disabling redirects. The allow-list fires once on the initial URL, but `requests` follows 3xx by default — a redirect into `http://169.254.169.254/...`, `http://localhost`, or another blocked target bypasses the guard and pulls the response back into the calling context. CVE-2026-41481 is the in-tree example (langchain-text-splitters < 1.1.2). Same shape applies in any agent-tooling code that does validate→fetch without `allow_redirects=False` / `follow_redirects=False` / `redirect: 'manual'`.
rules.remediation: Disable redirect following on the fetch call: `requests.get(url, allow_redirects=False)`, `httpx.get(url, follow_redirects=False)`, `fetch(url, { redirect: 'manual' })`. Or revalidate the URL on every redirect hop. For `langchain-text-splitters`, bump to >= 1.1.2.
rules.title: Validate-then-fetch SSRF (redirects enabled past allow-list)

rules.description: Source code calls `ToolNode([...])` (or `ToolNode(some_list)`) with a positional list rather than the documented `ToolNode(tools=[...])` keyword form. langgraph-prebuilt 1.0.11 (2026-04-24) regressed and silently coerces a positional list into a single-tool node, dropping every tool past the first and producing message-loop bugs in agents that depend on tool-routing behaviour.
rules.remediation: Switch every `ToolNode([t1, t2, ...])` to `ToolNode(tools=[t1, t2, ...])`. The codemod at `agent_audit_kit/autofix/langgraph_toolnode.py` rewrites the trivial shape; `aak suggest --apply-trivial --rule AAK-LANGGRAPH-TOOLNODE-LIST-REGRESSION-001` will run it (queued for v0.4.0).
rules.title: langgraph.prebuilt.ToolNode positional-list misuse

rules.description: A dependency uses a copyleft license (AGPL, SSPL) that may impose obligations on your project.
rules.remediation: Review license obligations. Consider replacing with permissively-licensed alternatives.
rules.title: Copyleft license (AGPL/SSPL) in dependency

rules.description: A dependency has no declared license, creating legal uncertainty about usage rights.
rules.remediation: Contact the maintainer to clarify licensing or replace with a properly licensed alternative.
rules.title: Dependency with no declared license

rules.description: A dependency has been flagged for DMCA/IP violations or contains leaked proprietary code.
rules.remediation: Remove the flagged dependency immediately.
rules.title: DMCA-flagged package detected

rules.description: A Python manifest (`requirements*.txt`, `pyproject.toml`, `Pipfile*`, `poetry.lock`, `uv.lock`) pins `litellm` at a version below 1.83.7. BerriAI/litellm shipped the CVE-2026-30623 fix in v1.83.7 on 2026-04-30. AAK-MCP-STDIO-CMD-INJ-001 already covers the source-side architectural shape; this pin-only rule complements it by surfacing a discrete finding for consumers who run pin-check mode and need an actionable manifest fix. Wired into `aak fix --cve` so the auto-fixer can rewrite requirements*.txt entries to `litellm>=1.83.7`.
rules.remediation: Bump `litellm` to >= 1.83.7. `aak fix --cve` rewrites a requirements*.txt pin in place; `pyproject.toml` / lockfile edits should go through the project's normal dependency-update workflow.
rules.title: LiteLLM pin floor for CVE-2026-30623 (<1.83.7)

rules.description: A vision-language pipeline calls `lmdeploy.serve.vl_engine.*` (or framework-equivalent) preprocessing helpers with a URL argument that is not validated against an allow-list. CVE-2026-33626 (GHSA-only at time of v0.3.6 cut — NVD enrichment pending) documents this exact shape: an attacker submits an image URL that points at a private endpoint, the loader fetches it server-side, and the response is processed by the VL pipeline. Same SSRF class as AAK-LANGCHAIN-SSRF-REDIR-001 but tied to the VL image loader.
rules.remediation: Wrap the URL with the same SSRF guard you use for any other fetch: validate the resolved IP against an allow-list, disable redirects, and pin the resolved IP for the actual request. Bump `lmdeploy` to the patched release (see GHSA for the exact version once NVD enrichment lands).
rules.title: LMDeploy VL image loader fetches user-controlled URLs without allow-list

rules.description: A `@tool`-decorated function parameter flows into logger.info / print / sys.stdout.write / console.log without stripping control characters (\r, \n, \x1b) first. CVE-2026-6494 (AAP MCP, CVSS 5.3 MEDIUM, CWE-117) lets an attacker forge log entries and inject ANSI escape sequences to socially engineer an operator.
rules.remediation: Strip \r\n\x1b (or accept only printable ASCII) before logging anything derived from tool input. Prefer structured logging (JSON/logfmt) so log consumers aren't confused by forged lines.
rules.title: MCP tool logs caller-controlled input without CRLF/ANSI sanitization

rules.description: .claude-plugin/marketplace.json lacks a signature or integrity hash field. An attacker with write access to the marketplace can replace the plugin bundle with no detection.
rules.remediation: Add a Sigstore signature or subresource-integrity hash to each plugin entry. Verify on install.
rules.title: Unsigned marketplace.json manifest

rules.description: A plugin's manifest grants access to filesystem, network, shell exec, or user-credential surfaces. Broad permissions should be opt-in after a clear user prompt.
rules.remediation: Trim permissions to the minimum the plugin needs. Review any 'fs:*' or 'shell:exec' entries.
rules.title: Plugin permission set grants broad access

rules.description: A plugin entry uses a name that is one edit distance from a popular upstream (e.g. 'anthropic', 'langchain', 'mcp'). Typosquatting is the single highest-volume supply-chain attack vector in 2026.
rules.remediation: Rename the plugin or flag it for manual review. Cross-reference against a known-upstream list.
rules.title: Plugin name typosquats a well-known package

rules.description: A plugin entry pins to a branch (main/master) or tag without commit SHA. The maintainer (or an attacker with write access) can silently change plugin behavior post-install — the 'maintainer takeover' pattern from the June 2024 xz incident and its 2026 re-runs.
rules.remediation: Pin to an immutable commit SHA. Re-pin during a reviewed dependency bump.
rules.title: Plugin source pins to a mutable git ref

rules.description: An MCP server uses HTTP transport (url field) without any authentication headers. Unauthenticated remote servers can be MITM'd or spoofed.
rules.remediation: Add OAuth 2.1 bearer token or API key header authentication.
rules.title: Remote MCP server without authentication

rules.description: An MCP server command contains shell metacharacters or shell wrappers (sh -c, bash -c). This enables command injection via argument composition.
rules.remediation: Use direct executable paths without shell wrappers.
rules.title: MCP server command runs with shell expansion

rules.description: Hardcoded secrets found in mcpServers env block. Secrets in project-scoped MCP config are committed to git.
rules.remediation: Use environment variable references or a secrets manager.
rules.title: MCP server environment exposes secrets

rules.description: More than 10 MCP servers in a single config. Large tool surface increases attack surface.
rules.remediation: Audit and remove unnecessary servers. Pin to minimum required set.
rules.title: Excessive number of MCP servers declared

rules.description: The command uses npx, uvx, bunx, or pnpx which fetches the latest version from a registry at runtime, vulnerable to typosquatting and dependency confusion.
rules.remediation: Pin exact package versions or use locally installed packages.
rules.title: MCP server uses npx/uvx to fetch and execute remote packages

rules.description: The command uses a relative path that can be hijacked via PATH manipulation.
rules.remediation: Use absolute paths for MCP server executables.
rules.title: MCP server command uses relative path

rules.description: Package name in args lacks @version suffix when using npx/uvx. Unpinned packages can silently update to malicious versions.
rules.remediation: Pin with @x.y.z suffix, e.g., @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem@2025.1.1
rules.title: MCP server lacks version pinning in args

rules.description: The headersHelper field executes arbitrary shell commands to generate headers. A malicious repo can exfiltrate data via header generation.
rules.remediation: Avoid headersHelper in project-scoped configs. Use static headers or OAuth flows instead.
rules.title: MCP server headersHelper executes arbitrary commands

rules.description: The MCP server URL points to localhost or internal network addresses, which may expose internal services (SSRF pattern).
rules.remediation: Ensure local MCP servers are intentional and document the trust assumption.
rules.title: MCP server URL points to localhost/internal network

rules.description: An MCP server is configured with filesystem root (/) or home directory access, allowing unrestricted file operations.
rules.remediation: Restrict filesystem access to specific project directories only.
rules.title: MCP server config allows arbitrary filesystem root access

rules.description: A remote MCP server exposes an HTTP handler with no authentication check. Per MCP spec 2025-11-25 all remote servers must require OAuth 2.1 or an equivalent bearer credential. Matches the CVE-2026-33032 pattern where /mcp_message was exposed without the auth middleware that /mcp used.
rules.remediation: Apply the same auth middleware to every MCP HTTP route. Do not branch on method or path before enforcing auth.
rules.title: Remote MCP server handler lacks authentication middleware

rules.description: An MCP server's IP allowlist configuration defaults to an empty list, which its middleware interprets as 'allow all'. This is the exact CVE-2026-33032 root cause. Tight-by-default is required.
rules.remediation: Default to deny-all; require explicit allowlist entries. Reject empty allowlists at startup.
rules.title: MCP server default IP allowlist is empty (allow-all)

rules.description: An MCP HTTP endpoint sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin to '*' while also returning credentials/tokens. This allows hostile origins to read MCP responses from a victim browser session.
rules.remediation: Restrict CORS to an explicit origin allowlist. Never combine '*' with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true.
rules.title: Wildcard CORS on MCP endpoint

rules.description: An MCP client or server expects authentication tokens in URL query parameters. Query parameters land in server access logs, browser history, and referer headers, making this a token-leak vector.
rules.remediation: Pass tokens in the Authorization header, never in query params.
rules.title: Auth token transmitted via URL query parameter

rules.description: An MCP server exposes a resource/file handler that passes user-supplied paths to open()/fs.readFile without normalization or allowlist checks. 2,614 MCP implementations surveyed; 82% had this class of issue.
rules.remediation: Resolve the requested path, reject '..' components, and verify the final path is under an explicit root directory.
rules.title: Path traversal in MCP resource handler

rules.description: An MCP server endpoint accepts request bodies without a maximum-size limit. This enables token-cost denial-of-service and memory exhaustion.
rules.remediation: Set a per-endpoint max body size (e.g. 1 MiB by default) and reject requests that exceed it.
rules.title: Unbounded prompt/argument size on MCP endpoint

rules.description: An MCP server configuration binds to plain HTTP without TLS. MCP spec 2025-11-25 requires Streamable HTTP over TLS for remote servers.
rules.remediation: Bind only to HTTPS. Terminate TLS at a trusted proxy or use the server's native TLS support. Reject plain-HTTP binds in production mode.
rules.title: MCP server accepts HTTP (non-TLS) in production config

rules.description: An MCP server endpoint does not declare rate limiting. Unrestricted access allows credential stuffing and enumeration attacks.
rules.remediation: Add per-IP and per-token rate limits. Reject bursts above the limit with 429.
rules.title: Missing rate limiting on MCP endpoint

rules.description: An MCP handler performs work (e.g. db lookups, external calls) before verifying authentication. This reveals existence/shape of protected resources via timing and error channels.
rules.remediation: Enforce authentication as the first step of every handler. Do not branch on caller input before the auth check.
rules.title: MCP auth check runs after side-effect

rules.description: Two MCP HTTP routes share a single handler but only one is wrapped in auth middleware. The second route inherits the tool surface without the auth check. This is the CVE-2026-33032 bypass pattern.
rules.remediation: Apply auth middleware at the router level, not per-route. Or, wrap the shared handler in the auth check.
rules.title: MCP handler shares routing with an unauthenticated path

rules.description: CVE-2026-27825 (CVSS 9.1): a Jira/Confluence field (`issue.fields.*`, `issue.description`, `comment.body`, `page.content`) flows from a tool handler into `subprocess.run/Popen/check_output`, `os.system`, or `os.popen` without input validation. Hacker News + The Hacker News (2026-04-22) documented public PoC; Atlassian is in every enterprise stack so treat any unpinned `mcp-atlassian` install as exposed.
rules.remediation: Pin `mcp-atlassian` to the patched version. Until the bump is in, wrap every Jira/Confluence field with an allow-list or shlex.quote before passing into subprocess. Consider front-running the agent surface with a redaction proxy.
rules.title: mcp-atlassian Jira/Confluence content reaches subprocess sink

rules.description: CVE-2026-27826 (CVSS 8.2): companion bug to CVE-2026-27825 — Jira/Confluence field content flows into `open(... 'w')`, `Path.write_text`, `shutil.move/copy` without validation. Lower blast radius than the subprocess variant but trivially weaponisable for path traversal + data planting.
rules.remediation: Same as CVE-2026-27825: pin `mcp-atlassian` to patched. For file-writes, additionally enforce a path allow-list rooted at the agent's tenant directory.
rules.title: mcp-atlassian Jira/Confluence content reaches file-write sink

rules.description: A registered MCP tool (Python `@mcp.tool` / `@server.tool`, TS `server.tool(...)`, Java `@Tool`, Rust `#[mcp_tool]`) carries a description containing imperative override language ('ignore previous', 'always call', 'this tool must be invoked first', 'supersedes all other tools') or a universal-suffix token from the FHI corpus (`agent_audit_kit/data/fhi_universal_suffixes.txt`). Function-Hijacking attacks steer the LLM planner into picking a malicious tool first regardless of intent — arXiv 2604.20994 reports 70-100% ASR on BFCL.
rules.remediation: Audit tool registration sites for descriptions that try to command the planner. Reject tools whose descriptions include directives like 'ignore previous instructions' or 'always invoke first'. Refresh the suffix corpus regularly with `aak corpus update --fhi`.
rules.title: MCP tool description carries adversarial-suffix shape

rules.description: CVE-2026-23744 (CVSS 9.8) in mcp-inspector ≤ 1.4.2. The preset-only entry from v0.3.5 caught configured presence; this rule catches forks that vendored or `node_modules`-pinned the vulnerable code regardless of declared dependency. Path-match on `vendor/mcpjam-inspector/**`, `node_modules/@mcpjam/inspector/**`, any `**/mcpjam-inspector/**` plus the unique `inspectorServer.handle(...)` call shape.
rules.remediation: Bump `@mcpjam/inspector` to >= 1.4.3 in package.json AND remove any vendored copies. Do not patch in-tree — rebase onto the published patched release.
rules.title: Vendored mcpjam-inspector fork carries CVE-2026-23744

rules.description: A function fetches a remote URL (`requests.get` / `httpx.get` / `urllib.request.urlopen` / `fetch`) and pipes the JSON / text return value into `StdioServerParameters(...)` or `new StdioClientTransport({...})` in the same function or one frame deep. The OX MCP April-2026 disclosure plus Cloudflare's MCP-defender reframe both call this out as the canonical supply-chain inversion: a marketplace compromise becomes client-side RCE on every consumer at the next refresh. Suppress with an entry in `.aak-mcp-marketplace-trust.yml`.
rules.remediation: Never feed a fetched marketplace manifest directly into `StdioServerParameters`. Cache the response, sign it, verify the signature on load, and pin `command` to a constant binary path regardless of what the manifest says. If the manifest URL is trusted (e.g. an internal artifact registry), add it to `.aak-mcp-marketplace-trust.yml` with a `justification:` field.
rules.title: MCP server config fetched from a marketplace URL and spawned

rules.description: A Python function calls `StdioServerParameters(command=..., args=...)` from `mcp.client.stdio` / `modelcontextprotocol.client` while also reading from a network-controlled source (request body, fetched JSON, environment variable wired to a webhook, untrusted YAML). The OX MCP April-2026 architectural class makes this exploitable: the SDK executes whatever ends up in `command`/`args` verbatim. See AAK-STDIO-001 for the broader sink-pattern detector; this rule is the SDK-named-API config-side counterpart.
rules.remediation: Never build `StdioServerParameters.command` / `.args` from a network-controlled value. Pin `command` to a constant binary path and validate `args` against an allow-list. If a tenant must pick the server, look the choice up in a server-side allow-list keyed by tenant identity, not by a free-form string in the request.
rules.title: MCP StdioServerParameters built from network-controlled input (Python)

rules.description: A TypeScript / JavaScript file constructs `new StdioClientTransport({ command, args })` from `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client/stdio` shortly after a network-controlled source (`req.body`, `await fetch(...).then(...)`, `process.env.<NETWORK_VAR>`, `JSON.parse(...)`). Same OX MCP April-2026 class as AAK-MCP-STDIO-CMD-INJ-001.
rules.remediation: Pin `command` to a constant binary path; validate `args` against an allow-list before passing them into the transport. Never feed fetched JSON or `req.body` directly into the transport options.
rules.title: MCP StdioClientTransport built from network-controlled input (TypeScript)

rules.description: A Java file constructs `StdioServerParameters.Builder().command(...).args(...).build()` from `io.modelcontextprotocol.sdk.client.stdio` after a network-controlled source (`HttpServletRequest`, `RestTemplate.getForObject`, `WebClient`, `ObjectMapper.readValue(...)`, `System.getenv(...)`). Same OX MCP April-2026 class as AAK-MCP-STDIO-CMD-INJ-001.
rules.remediation: Pin `command()` to a constant; validate `args()` against an allow-list. If using Spring, prefer `@Value`-injected configuration over per-request resolution.
rules.title: MCP StdioServerParameters built from network-controlled input (Java)

rules.description: A Rust file invokes `tokio::process::Command::new(...)` or `std::process::Command::new(...)` in a module that imports `mcp_sdk` / `modelcontextprotocol` after a network-controlled source (`reqwest`, `serde_json::from_str`, `std::env::var`, `hyper::body`, `actix_web::web::Json`, `axum::extract::Json`). Same OX MCP April-2026 class. NOTE: this rule is regex-only until #22 lands tree-sitter-rust; expect ~10% false-positive rate on macro-heavy code.
rules.remediation: Pin the `Command::new(...)` argument to a constant binary path and validate any subsequent `.arg(...)` values. Or move the process-spawn out of the request path entirely.
rules.title: MCP STDIO command spawned from network-controlled input (Rust)

rules.description: Project depends on `mcp-framework` at a version < 0.2.22, or a TS/JS file implements an MCP HTTP transport that concatenates request body chunks into a string without consulting Content-Length or a `maxMessageSize` guard. CVE-2026-39313 lets an unauthenticated attacker crash the server with a single large POST to /mcp by exhausting process memory. Fixed in 0.2.22.
rules.remediation: Upgrade `mcp-framework` to 0.2.22 or newer. For custom transports, enforce a hard body-size cap before accumulating chunks — reject early when `Content-Length` exceeds your `maxMessageSize`.
rules.title: mcp-framework < 0.2.22 HTTP-body DoS

rules.description: Two routes matching the MCP endpoint pattern (`/mcp`, `/mcp_message`, `/mcp/messages`, `/mcp[_-]invoke`, `/mcp[_-]tool`, ...) are declared in the same file, but one has no auth middleware while its twin does. This is the exact CVE-2026-33032 shape nginx-ui 2.3.4 patched and which VulnCheck KEV-listed on 2026-04-13 as actively exploited (CVSS 9.8). ~2,689 Shodan instances were exposed at disclosure; any network-adjacent caller can invoke the protected tools with zero credentials.
rules.remediation: Apply the same auth middleware to every MCP endpoint in a file. For Gin, use a `router.Use(AuthRequired())` group and mount all MCP routes inside it. For FastAPI, share a single `Depends(auth)` dependency across `@app.post('/mcp*')` decorators. For Express, create an `mcpRouter.use(authMw)` and mount it once.
rules.title: MCP route twin-asymmetry: auth middleware missing on sibling route (MCPwn, CVE-2026-33032)

rules.description: A dependency pin targets mcp-neo4j-cypher earlier than 0.6.0, or source code sets read_only=True while issuing CALL apoc.* / db.cypher.runWrite procedures. CVE-2026-35402 (CVSS 2.3 LOW, but integrity-critical) lets attackers bypass the read-only mode and execute arbitrary writes or SSRF.
rules.remediation: Upgrade mcp-neo4j-cypher to 0.6.0 or later. In source code, stop relying on read_only=True as a security boundary when APOC procedures are in scope; deny-list apoc.* at the query layer.
rules.title: mcp-neo4j-cypher < 0.6.0 APOC read-only bypass

rules.description: Project depends on `next-ai-draw-io` at a version below 0.4.15. CVE-2026-40608 is a body-accumulation OOM in the embedded HTTP sidecar's /api/state, /api/restore and /api/history-svg handlers — the entire request body is concatenated into a JavaScript string without a size cap, so a single ~500 MiB POST exhausts V8 heap and crashes the MCP server. Same DoS class as AAK-MCPFRAME-001. Fixed in 0.4.15.
rules.remediation: Upgrade `next-ai-draw-io` to 0.4.15 or later. For custom transports that replicate the pattern, enforce a hard body-size cap before accumulating chunks and reject early when `Content-Length` exceeds the cap.
rules.title: next-ai-draw-io < 0.4.15 body-accumulation DoS

rules.description: An OAuth 2.1 client flow does not use PKCE. PKCE is mandatory for all MCP remote server clients per spec 2025-11-25, including confidential clients.
rules.remediation: Add a code_verifier/code_challenge pair to every authorization request. Use code_challenge_method=S256.
rules.title: OAuth flow without PKCE

rules.description: An OAuth client sets code_challenge_method=plain (or omits it). S256 is mandatory; 'plain' leaks the verifier to anyone with access to the authorization request.
rules.remediation: Set code_challenge_method=S256 and derive code_challenge as BASE64URL(SHA256(code_verifier)).
rules.title: PKCE using the plain challenge method

rules.description: An MCP server receives a bearer token from one identity and forwards it to a downstream service without re-minting. This is the 'confused deputy' shape banned by OAuth 2.1 BCPs.
rules.remediation: Use token-exchange (RFC 8693) or a service account to call downstream services. Never forward a user token across trust boundaries.
rules.title: OAuth token passthrough between tenants

rules.description: An OAuth client registers a wildcard, localhost-with-any-port, or overly-broad redirect_uri. Attackers can hijack authorization codes via dangling subdomains or open redirectors.
rules.remediation: Register exact-match redirect URIs only. No wildcards.
rules.title: Wildcard or overly-broad redirect_uri

rules.description: An MCP remote server accepts plain Bearer tokens on a flow that MCP spec 2025-11-25 flags for DPoP (Demonstrating Proof of Possession) or mTLS-bound tokens. A stolen Bearer token is fully replayable.
rules.remediation: Require DPoP proofs or mTLS-bound tokens for high-privilege flows. Validate the token's cnf claim.
rules.title: Bearer token used where DPoP or mTLS is required

rules.description: The project depends on an agent-platform SDK (context-ai, langsmith, helicone, langfuse, humanloop, MCP SDK). Informational finding so reviewers audit the vendor's OAuth-scope footprint before merging. Raised to MEDIUM because the April 19 2026 Vercel × Context.ai incident showed a single vendor compromise can turn into a production breach via transitive OAuth grants.
rules.remediation: Pin the SDK to an exact version, audit the OAuth scopes it requests, and keep any deployment-level grants (Vercel, GCP, Workspace) in a secrets vault — never in a committed env file. See Vercel's bulletin for sensitive-env-var guidance: https://vercel.com/kb/bulletin/vercel-april-2026-security-incident
rules.title: Repo depends on a third-party agent-platform SDK

rules.description: A config file in this repo grants a non-first-party Google OAuth client broad Workspace scopes (admin.*, cloud-platform, drive, directory.*, gmail.modify/send). The April 19 2026 Vercel × Context.ai breach is the template: a single compromised third-party OAuth app with deployment-level scopes let attackers pivot into production. Explicitly allowlist trusted client IDs in `.aak-oauth-trust.yml`.
rules.remediation: Review the granted scopes — drop admin.* / cloud-platform where possible. Add every legitimate third-party client_id to `.aak-oauth-trust.yml` under `trusted_client_ids:`. Rotate the consent if the client isn't recognised.
rules.title: Third-party OAuth client granted broad Workspace scopes

rules.description: `OpenClawAgent(role=...)` is unset, None, or sourced from untrusted input without `assert_role_allowlisted(role)`. IronPlate's 2026-04-07 weekly intel flagged a CVSS 9.9 privilege-escalation in OpenClaw where a missing/forgable role default lets a prompt-borne agent escalate to admin actions. Provisional rule pending a public CVE assignment.
rules.remediation: Always pass an explicit, allow-listed role to OpenClawAgent. Wrap the assignment with `aak.checks.openclaw.assert_role_allowlisted(role)`. Pin the OpenClaw version once the upstream patch ships; the AAK rule will auto-promote from `provisional` to `confirmed` when `scripts/refresh_openclaw_status.py` finds a CVE ID.
rules.title: OpenClaw agent role missing or attacker-influenced

rules.description: Meta / informational rule that surfaces the project's static CVE coverage map (`agent_audit_kit/data/ox-cve-manifest.json`). Drives the OX-coverage badge endpoint and the `aak coverage --source ox` CLI; never fires findings on user code.
rules.remediation: Run `aak coverage --source ox` to see which OX-disclosed CVEs are covered by AAK rules. The manifest is regenerated on every release and powers the `OX coverage` badge in README.
rules.title: Project OX-disclosed CVE coverage manifest

rules.description: Tool description contains invisible Unicode characters (zero-width joiners, RTL overrides, invisible separators) that could hide malicious instructions.
rules.remediation: Remove invisible characters from tool descriptions.
rules.title: Invisible Unicode characters in tool description

rules.description: Tool description contains prompt injection patterns such as 'ignore previous instructions', 'system:', or role-switching directives.
rules.remediation: Remove injection patterns from tool descriptions.
rules.title: Prompt injection patterns in tool description

rules.description: Tool description references other tools by name, potentially triggering chain calls (e.g., 'before using this tool, first call X').
rules.remediation: Tool descriptions should be self-contained and not reference other tools.
rules.title: Cross-tool reference in tool description

rules.description: Tool description contains base64, hex, or URL-encoded content that could hide malicious instructions.
rules.remediation: All tool description content should be plain text.
rules.title: Encoded content in tool description

rules.description: Tool description exceeds 500 characters, which increases the surface area for hidden instructions.
rules.remediation: Keep tool descriptions concise and under 500 characters.
rules.title: Excessive tool description length

rules.description: Tool description contains URLs or file paths that could direct the agent to access external resources.
rules.remediation: Remove URLs and file paths from tool descriptions.
rules.title: URL or file path in tool description

rules.description: Meta / informational rule that surfaces AAK's static coverage of the public Prisma AIRS 3.0 attack catalog. Drives `aak coverage --source prisma-airs` and the published coverage matrix. Never fires findings on user code.
rules.remediation: Run `aak coverage --source prisma-airs` to see how AAK's static ruleset maps onto Palo Alto's published Prisma AIRS attack catalog. The map is hand-curated; entries flagged `status: catalog-private` are not publicly disclosed and intentionally absent from AAK coverage.
rules.title: Prisma AIRS catalog coverage manifest

rules.description: A pricing function (set_price / quote / bid / list_price / negotiate / price_item / compute_price) calls an LLM with a templated `model=` argument and is not gated by `@aak.parity.check` (or equivalent). Anthropic's 2026-04-26 Project Deal experiment found Opus sellers earned $2.68/item more than Haiku sellers despite identical buyer ratings (4.06 vs 4.05). This is OWASP LLM09 (overreliance / economic harm) — without per-tier parity assertions, deploying multiple model tiers behind the same pricing surface produces silent revenue / cost drift across customer cohorts.
rules.remediation: Wrap pricing functions with `@aak.parity.check(dimensions=['model'], metric='price', max_drift_pct=1.5)` and run `aak parity report` in CI. The decorator records every invocation's (model, price) tuple and raises `ParityDriftError` if any per-tier mean drifts more than the configured threshold from the overall mean.
rules.title: Cross-tier LLM pricing without parity check (Project Deal class)

rules.description: A function pulls a title-like field from a GitHub event source (`pull_request.title`, `pull_request.head.ref`, `issue.title`, or env vars wired to the same) and feeds it into an LLM client call (`anthropic.messages.create`, `openai.chat.completions.create`, `genai.GenerativeModel.generate_content`, `langchain.*.invoke`) without an HTML-escape, allow-list, or hash on the title. Aonan Guan's 2026-04-25 Comment-and-Control disclosure (CVSS 9.4) showed an attacker-controlled PR title injects instructions the agent executes with its own credentials — credential theft demonstrated against Claude Code Security Review, Gemini CLI Action, and GitHub Copilot Agent.
rules.remediation: Wrap the title in `html.escape` (or `markupsafe.escape`), or validate against a strict allow-list, or hash it before interpolating into the prompt. For TS/JS, use the equivalent. For shell-style agents, prefer `shlex.quote`. The fix is structural — never interpolate untrusted GitHub event content into an LLM prompt.
rules.title: PR/issue title flows into LLM client without sanitiser

rules.description: A routine configuration declares tool permissions wider than what the same user has in interactive Claude Code. A routine running non-interactively at 3am with admin-level tools is an excessive-agency risk (OWASP ASI05).
rules.remediation: Mirror routine permissions from the interactive grant. Require re-prompt for elevation.
rules.title: Routine grants broader permissions than interactive path

rules.description: A routine's cron expression, HTTP webhook URL, or GitHub event filter is built from a user-controlled value. Schedule injection can repurpose the routine at off-hours without review.
rules.remediation: Treat schedule expressions as static constants; never build them from runtime state.
rules.title: Routine schedule interpolates unsanitized input

rules.description: A routine runs tool calls but writes no run-log, making post-hoc audit impossible. An attacker with edit access to the routine file can run anything and delete the evidence.
rules.remediation: Route every routine's output (and tool-call trace) to an append-only log the routine cannot modify.
rules.title: Routine executes without audit trail

rules.description: A tool's definition (name, description, or input schema) has changed since it was last pinned. This could indicate a rug pull attack.
rules.remediation: Review the changes. If legitimate, re-pin with 'agent-audit-kit pin'. If suspicious, remove the server.
rules.title: Tool definition changed since last pin

rules.description: A new tool was added to an MCP server since the last pin. New tools should be reviewed before approval.
rules.remediation: Review the new tool's definition and permissions. Pin if approved.
rules.title: New tool added since last pin

rules.description: A previously pinned tool was removed from an MCP server. This could indicate covering tracks after an attack.
rules.remediation: Investigate why the tool was removed. Update pins if removal was intentional.
rules.title: Tool removed since last pin

rules.description: A repository whose name or pyproject keywords declare it as an MCP server ships without a top-level SECURITY.md AND without a `security_contact` entry in marketplace.json / pyproject.toml / package.json. Anthropic's April 2026 SECURITY.md guidance makes this the baseline expectation so researchers have a channel.
rules.remediation: Add SECURITY.md at the repo root with a disclosure email and response SLA; OR add `security_contact` to the project manifest.
rules.title: MCP server repo missing SECURITY.md or security_contact

rules.description: An Anthropic API key (sk-ant-*) was found in a project file. This allows full API access and billing abuse.
rules.remediation: Remove key, rotate immediately, use environment variables.
rules.title: Anthropic API key exposed

rules.description: An OpenAI API key (sk-*) was found in a project file.
rules.remediation: Remove key, rotate immediately, use environment variables.
rules.title: OpenAI API key exposed

rules.description: AWS access key ID or secret access key found in a project file.
rules.remediation: Use IAM roles, AWS SSO, or secrets manager.
rules.title: AWS credentials exposed

rules.description: A value assigned to a secret-like key has high Shannon entropy, indicating a likely credential or API key.
rules.remediation: Move to environment variables or secrets manager.
rules.title: Generic high-entropy secret

rules.description: A private key file (*.pem, *.key, id_rsa, etc.) or PEM content was found in the project.
rules.remediation: Remove private keys from repository, add to .gitignore.
rules.title: Private key file present

rules.description: A .env file exists but .gitignore does not contain a .env exclusion pattern.
rules.remediation: Add .env* to .gitignore.
rules.title: .env file not in .gitignore

rules.description: Hardcoded secret values found in mcpServers env blocks in non-.mcp.json files.
rules.remediation: Use environment variable references.
rules.title: Secret in MCP server environment block

rules.description: A GitHub or GitLab personal access token (ghp_*, glpat-*) was found in a project file.
rules.remediation: Remove token, rotate immediately, use environment variables.
rules.title: GitHub/GitLab personal access token exposed

rules.description: A Google Cloud service account key JSON file was found in the project.
rules.remediation: Remove key file, use workload identity or environment-based auth.
rules.title: Google Cloud service account key file

rules.description: A SKILL.md frontmatter or body declares a post-install or auto-run command (bash, curl, pipe-to-sh, wget). Skills should be declarative; arbitrary installation commands are a rug-pull risk.
rules.remediation: Remove the post-install command. If setup is genuinely required, document it for the user rather than auto-running it.
rules.title: SKILL.md contains a post-install / side-effect command

rules.description: A SKILL.md body contains hidden characters (bidi override, zero-width, tag-unicode) that render differently than they parse. This hides malicious tool-use instructions from human review.
rules.remediation: Remove U+200B-U+200F, U+202A-U+202E, U+E0000-U+E007F and similar invisible / bidi characters from skill text.
rules.title: SKILL.md uses unicode steganography in tool descriptions

rules.description: A SKILL.md references outbound HTTP tools (fetch/curl/request) combined with instructions to send local data (files, environment, credentials).
rules.remediation: Remove the exfil instruction. Skills that genuinely need outbound HTTP should declare it explicitly with a documented purpose.
rules.title: SKILL.md embeds data-exfiltration primitives

rules.description: A skill's frontmatter name or description mimics a well-known skill ('pdf', 'docx', 'frontend-design') but the body declares unrelated / hostile instructions. Description hijacking is the ToxicSkills 2026 signature pattern.
rules.remediation: Rename the skill to match its actual behavior. Cross-reference the name against the first-party skill directory.
rules.title: SKILL.md description hijacks a trusted skill name

rules.description: A SKILL.md frontmatter or YAML header embeds phrases targeted at the loading model ('ignore previous', 'you are now', 'system:'). These are prompt-injection probes rather than legitimate skill metadata.
rules.remediation: Remove the injection trigger. Skill frontmatter should only contain name, description, tags, and similar metadata.
rules.title: SKILL.md frontmatter contains prompt-injection triggers

rules.description: A splunk-mcp-server configuration (`inputs.conf`, `splunk-mcp.yaml`, or any file under `splunk-mcp/`) routes a token-bearing source into the `_internal` or `_audit` index, or names a sourcetype known to carry session tokens (`splunk_session`, `mcp_auth`, `bearer`). Distinct from AAK-SPLUNK-TOKLOG-001 which fires on log-sink taint at runtime — this rule fires on the configuration that *makes* the runtime leak inevitable. CVE-2026-20205 origin.
rules.remediation: Route token-bearing inputs to a redaction stage *before* the Splunk forwarder. Never write to `_internal` from the MCP server. Bump `splunk-mcp-server` to >= 1.0.3.
rules.title: splunk-mcp-server configured to write tokens to _internal / audit

rules.description: An MCP server, agent, or tool logs a session token, JWT, or Bearer credential through a generic log sink (logger.info / .warn / .error, print) without redaction. CVE-2026-20205 (splunk-mcp-server < 1.0.3) shipped this exact pattern — session tokens ended up in the Splunk `_internal` index, readable by anyone with index-read. Any token written to a log sink is also a supply-chain risk: the log file, shipper, and SIEM are now in scope for the token's blast radius.
rules.remediation: Redact token-shaped values before logging. Never interpolate a raw `Authorization`, `Bearer`, JWT, `splunkd_session`, or `st-` credential into a log message. Pin `splunk-mcp-server >= 1.0.3`.
rules.title: Session token written to log sink in cleartext

rules.description: An MCP tool handler fetches a URL provided by the caller with no host/scheme validation. This is the classic SSRF shape (CWE-918).
rules.remediation: Validate the scheme (https only), resolve the host, and reject private-range IPs (RFC 1918, 169.254.*, 127.*, ::1/128, fc00::/7).
rules.title: Unvalidated outbound HTTP in MCP tool handler

rules.description: An MCP tool handler forwards user-supplied URLs that could target 127.0.0.1/localhost/::1, reaching internal services bound to loopback.
rules.remediation: Block loopback and link-local addresses after DNS resolution.
rules.title: Localhost/loopback reachable from MCP tool

rules.description: An MCP tool accepts URLs that can reach 169.254.169.254 (AWS/Azure/GCP metadata) or metadata.google.internal, allowing exfiltration of instance credentials.
rules.remediation: Block 169.254.0.0/16 and metadata.google.internal at the HTTP client layer. Use a deny-by-default IP allowlist.
rules.title: Cloud metadata endpoint reachable via MCP tool

rules.description: An MCP tool follows HTTP redirects using the default client settings. An attacker can bypass initial host checks by returning a 3xx to an internal address. DNS rebinding works the same way.
rules.remediation: Disable automatic redirects, or re-run host validation on every hop. Cap total redirects at 3.
rules.title: Redirect chains followed without re-validation

rules.description: An MCP tool performs outbound HTTP but has no allowlist of permitted destinations. Deny-by-default with an explicit allowlist is the only reliable defense against SSRF chains.
rules.remediation: Maintain an explicit allowlist of hostnames; reject anything else.
rules.title: Missing SSRF allowlist on outbound fetch

rules.description: A function validates a URL via an SSRF guard, then performs a separate network fetch that triggers an independent DNS resolution. Between the two resolutions a malicious hostname can rotate from a public IP to a private/localhost/cloud-metadata IP (DNS rebinding) — bypassing the allow-list. CVE-2026-41488 (langchain-openai `_url_to_size`) is the canonical example. The fix is to resolve once, pin the IP, and reuse the same `Session` / `HTTPAdapter` for the fetch — or drive the allow-list check on the resolved IP instead of the hostname.
rules.remediation: Resolve the hostname once with `socket.getaddrinfo`, validate the resolved IP against the allow-list, then make the fetch over a `Session` / connection pinned to that IP (e.g. via `Host:` header + explicit IP, custom `HTTPAdapter`, or `pinned_ip`-style helper). Pin `langchain-openai >= 1.1.14`.
rules.title: Validate-then-fetch DNS-rebind / TOCTOU on URL allow-list

rules.description: A privacy policy / notice lacks the CCPA-lineage opt-out-of-sale language that Alabama DPPA, CCPA, CPRA, VCDPA, and the other 21 state comprehensive privacy laws converge on.
rules.remediation: Add a 'Do Not Sell / Share My Personal Information' section and a usable opt-out mechanism.
rules.title: Privacy doc missing 'do-not-sell' / opt-out-of-sale language

rules.description: A privacy policy does not describe the consumer's access, deletion, or portability rights — mandatory across every state comprehensive privacy law passed 2018-2026.
rules.remediation: Describe DSAR submission, 45-day cure window where applicable, and the portability format.
rules.title: Privacy doc missing access / deletion / portability rights

rules.description: A privacy policy does not expose a data-controller contact (DPO email / privacy@ / mailing address). Required by most state laws and a prerequisite for any DSAR.
rules.remediation: Add a privacy@ inbox and a postal mailing address for DSARs.
rules.title: Privacy doc missing data-controller contact

rules.description: User-controllable input flows into a STDIO command executor in an MCP server implementation — the architectural shape Ox Security traced through CVE-2026-30615 (Windsurf RCE) and nine other CVEs across ~200,000 exposed servers. Matches subprocess / os.system / os.popen / os.exec / eval / exec where an arg references a taint source (request params, stdin, @tool parameter, json.loads(stdin)). TS/JS variant: child_process.spawn / execa with {shell:true} or a request-derived command string.
rules.remediation: Never pass caller-controlled data into a shell. Use argv lists (subprocess.run([...]) without shell=True), allowlist the command set, and validate arguments against an explicit schema. For TS, pass argv as an array with shell:false and validate every element against a regex or allowlist.
rules.title: MCP STDIO command-injection (Ox architectural class)

rules.description: MCP server args contain package names without @x.y.z version pinning. Unpinned packages fetch latest at runtime, vulnerable to rug pull attacks.
rules.remediation: Pin to exact version. Example: @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem@2025.1.1
rules.title: MCP server package not pinned to exact version

rules.description: A package with known MCP/AI-agent-related vulnerabilities was found in the dependency tree.
rules.remediation: Update to patched version or remove dependency.
rules.title: Known vulnerable package in lockfile

rules.description: package.json has install scripts (preinstall, postinstall, etc.) that execute arbitrary commands during npm install.
rules.remediation: Audit install scripts. Use --ignore-scripts flag and run scripts manually after review.
rules.title: Dependency uses install scripts

rules.description: A package manifest exists but no lockfile was found. Without lockfiles, dependency versions float and can be silently updated.
rules.remediation: Generate and commit lockfile.
rules.title: No lockfile present

rules.description: More than 200 direct + transitive dependencies in lockfile. Each dependency is a trust decision.
rules.remediation: Audit and remove unused dependencies. Consider lighter alternatives.
rules.title: Dependency count exceeds threshold

rules.description: A dependency has a known vulnerability specifically affecting MCP/agent tooling.
rules.remediation: Update to patched version as listed in the vulnerability database.
rules.title: Dependency with known MCP-specific vulnerability

rules.description: A @tool function parameter is passed to os.system(), subprocess, or similar shell execution functions without sanitization.
rules.remediation: Sanitize all inputs. Use subprocess with shell=False and explicit argument lists.
rules.title: Tool parameter flows to shell command

rules.description: A @tool function parameter flows to eval(), exec(), or compile() enabling arbitrary code execution.
rules.remediation: Never pass user-controlled input to eval/exec. Use safe parsing alternatives.
rules.title: Tool parameter flows to eval/exec

rules.description: A @tool function parameter is used in open() or file path construction, enabling path traversal attacks.
rules.remediation: Validate and sanitize file paths. Use os.path.realpath() and verify against allowed directories.
rules.title: Tool parameter flows to file open

rules.description: A @tool function parameter is passed to requests.get/post or urllib, enabling SSRF attacks.
rules.remediation: Validate URLs against an allowlist. Block internal/private IP ranges.
rules.title: Tool parameter flows to HTTP request

rules.description: A @tool function parameter is used in cursor.execute() or similar database query functions via string formatting.
rules.remediation: Use parameterized queries. Never use f-strings or .format() for SQL.
rules.title: Tool parameter flows to SQL query

rules.description: A @tool function parameter is passed to pickle.loads(), yaml.load(), or similar deserialization functions.
rules.remediation: Use safe deserialization (yaml.safe_load, json.loads) instead of unsafe alternatives.
rules.title: Tool parameter flows to deserialization

rules.description: A @tool function has no type hints or input validation on its parameters.
rules.remediation: Add type hints and input validation to all tool function parameters.
rules.title: Tool function missing input validation

rules.description: A @tool function accesses more than 3 different dangerous sinks, indicating overly broad permissions.
rules.remediation: Split into smaller, focused tool functions with minimal privileges.
rules.title: Tool function with excessive dangerous sinks

rules.description: A task read endpoint returns task state based on the task ID alone. Any caller that guesses or enumerates a task ID gets another user's data (CWE-639).
rules.remediation: Verify the caller is the task owner (or has an explicit grant) before returning task state.
rules.title: MCP task read endpoint lacks per-task authorization

rules.description: A task record retains API keys / OAuth tokens after the task reaches a terminal state (completed/failed/cancelled). Long-lived credentials in persistent state are an obvious exfil target.
rules.remediation: Zeroize credential fields when the task transitions to a terminal state. Keep only what a post-mortem actually needs.
rules.title: MCP tasks persist credentials past completion

rules.description: A task record has no expiration and no cancellation endpoint. Orphaned tasks accumulate forever, including their inputs.
rules.remediation: Set a TTL on every task; expose a cancellation endpoint that zeroizes inputs and credentials.
rules.title: MCP task has no TTL or cancellation path

rules.description: Source code wires a social-platform reply sink (`tiktok_api.reply`, `instagrapi.direct.send`, `tweepy.API.update_status`, `discord.Message.reply`, etc.) to a user-content source (`comments.fetch`, webhook payload `text` field, media comments) without an `aak.review.human_in_loop()` / `human_in_the_loop()` / `require_approval()` gate.
